title: The First Week That Sticks: Onboarding That Builds Baristas
subtitle: Most new hires decide whether to stay within the first five shifts. Make those shifts count.

toc.label: Where Most Cafes Lose New Hires
When to Assess and How to Give Feedback

metrics.label: target new-hire attrition in first 3 months

sections.heading: Where Most Cafes Lose New Hires
sections.body:
The pattern is depressingly common. You spend two weeks finding a good candidate, offer them the job, and on day one you are short-staffed so you throw them on the machine during the morning rush. By day three they are stressed and confused. By week two they have quit, and you are back to square one.

Cafe turnover in the first 90 days is staggeringly high — in many operations, more than half of new hires leave within the first three months. The cost each time: recruitment advertising, interview time, training hours, the productivity drag on the team covering for the gap, and the customer experience that suffers while a position is unfilled or being learned.

The root cause is rarely the hire. It is the onboarding. Or rather, the absence of one.

A good onboarding system does three things:
1. **Makes people feel welcome** before it makes them feel competent
2. **Builds skills progressively** rather than dumping everything on day one
3. **Creates a feedback loop** so problems surface early, not at the resignation

sections.heading: Day One: Belonging Before Skills
sections.body:
The first day is not about the espresso machine. It is about making the new person feel like they belong.

Before they arrive:
- Their name is on the rota (not pencilled in as "new starter")
- Their apron, login, and locker are ready
- The team knows they are coming and their name
- A buddy is assigned (more on this below)

When they arrive:
- **Tour first.** Where everything is — not just the machine, but the toilet, the staff area, where to put their bag, where the first aid kit lives.
- **Introductions.** By name, with something personal. "This is Karolina, she's been with us two years and she's our best closer."
- **The cafe story.** Why the cafe exists, what you care about, what makes it different. People want to work somewhere with purpose, even if it is a small, simple purpose.
- **Expectations.** What does a good shift look like here? What are the non-negotiables? Not a lecture — a conversation.

Day one should end with the new hire knowing three things:
1. People here are friendly and I feel welcome
2. I know where things are and I will not feel lost tomorrow
3. I understand what this place is about

They should NOT be expected to make drinks, serve customers solo, or close a till on day one. Observation and orientation only.

sections.heading: The Five-Shift Progression
sections.body:
Structure the first five shifts as a deliberate learning progression (T070-01). Each shift has a focus, building on the last.

**Shift 1: Watch and learn.**
Shadow the buddy. Observe the workflow. Help with simple tasks — clearing tables, restocking napkins, washing up. Learn the menu by reading it and asking questions. No pressure, no performance.

**Shift 2: Hands-on basics.**
Operate the till with supervision. Learn the drink menu categories. Start steaming milk (practice jugs, not live orders). Learn the cleaning routine.

**Shift 3: Supervised service.**
Make simple drinks under supervision — filter coffee, tea, hot chocolate. Take customer orders at the till. Start learning the food prep basics.

**Shift 4: Espresso with support.**
Pull shots and steam milk for real orders, with the buddy checking quality. Handle straightforward customer interactions independently. Learn opening or closing procedures.

**Shift 5: Confidence shift.**
Work a section of the bar or counter semi-independently. The buddy is nearby but not hovering. Handle a short peak period with support. Debrief at the end — what went well, what felt hard.

This progression assumes the new hire has some hospitality experience. For complete beginners, extend it to seven or eight shifts. For experienced baristas joining from another cafe, you can compress it — but never skip day one orientation.

Use T070-02 (Onboarding Tracker) to check off each competency as it is demonstrated. This is not bureaucracy — it is a safety net that ensures nothing gets missed.

sections.heading: Buddy Systems That Actually Work
sections.body:
Assigning a buddy is easy. Making the buddy system effective takes thought.

**Choose the right buddy.** Not your best barista — your most patient team member. Technical skill can be taught; the ability to explain things calmly and make someone feel comfortable cannot. The buddy should be someone who remembers what it felt like to be new.

**Brief the buddy.** They need to know what is expected:
- Stay close for the first three shifts — do not wander off during a rush
- Explain the "why" behind routines, not just the "what"
- Let the new person make small mistakes, then coach immediately
- Report back to the manager daily: what went well, what needs more time

**Protect the buddy's workload.** If you expect someone to train a new hire *and* run a full section during peak, neither job gets done well. Schedule an extra person, or reduce the buddy's responsibilities during training shifts.

**Rotate after week one.** The buddy relationship should evolve. After the first week, the new hire should start working with different team members to learn different styles and build relationships across the team.

The buddy is not the manager's substitute. The manager should still check in with the new hire daily during the first week — a two-minute conversation is enough. "How are you finding it? Anything confusing? Anything you need?"

sections.heading: When to Assess and How to Give Feedback
sections.body:
Assessment during onboarding should feel like coaching, not testing.

Use T070-03 (Onboarding Quality Scorecard) to structure feedback at three points:

**End of week one:** Can they navigate the space? Do they know the menu? Can they operate the till? Are they comfortable interacting with customers? This is a pass/fail on basics — if someone cannot manage these after five shifts, there is a problem.

**End of week two:** Can they make the core drink menu to standard? Can they prep basic food items? Do they handle a mini-rush without freezing? This is where technical competency starts to matter.

**Day 30:** Can they open or close? Can they work independently through a peak period? Do they troubleshoot basic issues (grinder needs adjusting, display needs restocking) without being told? This is full competency.

Feedback rules:
- **Immediate.** Correct technique in the moment, not at a meeting next week. "Hey, your milk is a bit too hot — aim for here on the thermometer" takes five seconds and prevents a bad habit.
- **Specific.** Not "your coffee needs work." Instead: "Your shots are running 22 seconds — let's go a click finer on the grind."
- **Balanced.** Name what is going well first. New hires are anxious; leading with positives makes correction easier to receive.
- **Private for problems.** Never correct someone in front of customers. Step aside, fix it, move on.

If someone is struggling at the week-two checkpoint, do not wait. Have an honest, kind conversation: "Here is where you are, here is where you need to be, here is how we will get you there." Give them a clear path and a deadline. Most people rise to clear expectations.

sections.heading: The 30-Day Check-In
sections.body:
At 30 days, sit down for a proper conversation. Not a formal review — a check-in. Use T070-05 (Post-Onboarding Debrief Survey) to structure it, but keep it conversational.

Ask:
- **"How do you feel about the job so far?"** Open-ended. Let them talk. You will learn more from their hesitations than their answers.
- **"What has been harder than expected?"** This surfaces training gaps and workflow friction you might not see.
- **"Is there anything about the way we work that surprised you?"** New eyes see things the team has normalised. Their observations are valuable.
- **"What would make your shifts better?"** This is not a wish list — it is intelligence. A new hire who says "I never know what to prep" is telling you your prep system is invisible.
- **"Do you see yourself here in six months?"** Direct. If the answer is hesitant, dig deeper now rather than being surprised by a resignation later.

Share your assessment too. Tell them what they are doing well and where they need to develop next. Set one or two goals for the next 30 days — not a long list, just the next step.

The 30-day check-in is also your early warning system for retention. If a new hire is already disengaged, unhappy with the team, or struggling with the pace, this is your window to intervene. After 60 days, they have usually already decided.

Document the conversation briefly. Not for HR bureaucracy — so you can refer back to it at 60 and 90 days and show progress.
